title: Therapeutic Options for Large B-Cell Lymphoma Relapsing After CD19-Directed CAR T-Cell Therapy
authors_year: El Warrak S, Kharfan-Dabaja MA, Iqbal M, Hamadani M, Chavez J, Mohty R. 2024
design: Narrative review synthesizing observational/retrospective studies and early-phase trials of salvage options after CD19 CAR-T failure in LBCL: CD19-directed agents (tafasitamab-lenalidomide, loncastuximab), polatuzumab, CD20xCD3 bispecific antibodies (epcoritamab, glofitamab, mosunetuzumab, others), allogeneic HCT, and re-treatment/next-generation CAR-T. Includes a proposed post-CAR-T treatment algorithm.
population: Adults with R/R LBCL who relapse or progress after CD19-directed CAR-T (axi-cel, tisa-cel, liso-cel). Post-CAR-T failure carries a poor prognosis (median OS ~6 months).
key_eligibility: Frames the population downstream of failed CAR-T — a distinct decision node where allo-HCT and bispecifics enter.
Allo-HCT is RESERVED for patients who relapse after CAR-T, are otherwise transplant-eligible, and achieve an objective response (CR or at least PR) to subsequent therapy.
Attainment of CR (or at least PR) prior to allografting is an explicit prerequisite for durable remission — mirrors PARMA-era chemosensitivity principle.
CD19-directed agents (tafa-len, loncastuximab) show markedly reduced efficacy after CD19 CAR-T failure (ORR ~17-34%, often CD19 antigen loss).
CD20xCD3 bispecifics (epcoritamab, glofitamab) retain meaningful activity post-CAR-T and can serve as a bridge to allo-HCT.
outcomes: Post-CAR-T failure median OS ~6 months. CD19 agents post-CAR-T: tafa-len/loncaT ORR ~17-34%, CR ~5-17%, short PFS. Bispecifics post-CAR-T: epcoritamab ORR 54.1%/CR 34.4% in prior-CAR-T subgroup; glofitamab CR 35% in prior-CAR-T. Allo-HCT after CAR-T failure (Zurko et al, N=88): 1-year OS 59%, PFS 45%, NRM 22%; disease status CR in 51% at transplant. Fried et al (N=39): 2-year OS 45%, PFS 31%, NRM 43%. Durable remission achievable only in selected, chemo/immuno-responsive, fit (median age <65) patients.
grounds_rubric_claims: Allogeneic HCT is reserved for LBCL patients who relapse after CAR-T (not used before/instead of CAR-T)
Allo-HCT after CAR-T requires achieving a CR (or at least PR) to subsequent therapy as a prerequisite for durable benefit
CD19-directed agents lose efficacy after CD19 CAR-T failure (antigen loss/downregulation); non-CD19 bispecifics are preferred and can bridge to allo-HCT
Post-CAR-T relapse is a poor-prognosis state (median OS ~6 months) warranting clinical-trial enrollment when available

verbatim_quotes.quote: Prior to the approval and widespread use of CAR T in R/R LBCL, allo-HCT was generally reserved for patients who relapsed after an auto-HCT and/or had failed multiple prior lines of therapy.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Allo-HCT is positioned as a later-line, post-cellular-therapy option — reserved, not front-loaded

verbatim_quotes.quote: One of the main limitations for allo-HCT is the prerequisite of achieving disease control in the form of CR or at least PR.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Chemosensitivity/response prerequisite for allo-HCT after CAR-T failure

verbatim_quotes.quote: relapsing after CAR Ts or failure to respond is generally associated with a poor prognosis with an anticipated median overall survival (OS) of approximately 6 months, at best
verbatim_quotes.supports: Post-CAR-T failure is a poor-prognosis decision node (median OS ~6 months)

caveats: Review-level (not a controlled trial); allo-HCT and bispecific data are largely retrospective/single-arm with selection bias toward younger, fitter, response-achieving patients (median age <65). No head-to-head data define optimal sequencing; authors recommend clinical-trial enrollment first, then bispecific/ADC/mAb (ideally targeting a non-CD19 antigen) with allo-HCT consolidation in eligible responders.
